answer:
We label the vertices 0 through 7 in order. A rotation by \(k\) steps sends vertex \(i\) to \(i+k \pmod{8}\).  
The condition means there exists a rotation \(k\) such that for every blue vertex \(b\), the vertex \(b+k\) is red. Equivalently, the set of blue vertices is a subset of the complement of itself shifted by \(k\), so the blue set must be contained in the red set after rotation. This is possible iff the blue vertices are all contained in some set of 4 vertices that are exactly half the octagon, because a rotation mapping blue to red requires the blue set size \(\le 4\) and the pattern must be periodic with period dividing 8.

The only way this works for a given coloring is if the blue vertices are a subset of a 4-vertex set that is invariant under some nontrivial rotation (i.e., a coset of a subgroup). For a regular
